lesson.menu_desc: Large Numbers, Percentages and Comparatives -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Numbers, Prices, and Contracts -- Comparatives and Big Numbers
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Walyson Ginaldo Silva | Aula 6 -- Numbers, Prices, and Contracts

listenings.text: Hi, this is Walyson. I am recording a quick note about a price quote I am preparing for a buyer in Europe. They asked for a quote for one container, so the volume is large. Our cost is high this season, so the margin is small. I will offer a rate of four thousand two hundred dollars per ton. That figure is a little lower than last month, because the market dropped by about five percent. If they order a bigger volume, I can give them a small discount, maybe three percent. I will send the quote and the invoice today, in dollars, the usual currency for coffee. I think our price is more competitive than our main rival, and it is cheaper than the offer they had last week. I really hope they accept the bid.

Hello, this is Marina with a short market update. Prices moved a lot this week. On Monday, the rate for premium coffee was three thousand nine hundred dollars per ton. By Friday, it had increased by eight percent, so it is now higher than last month. Cheaper grades also went up, but more slowly, only about two percent. Demand is strong, so the volume of orders is bigger than usual. My estimate is that prices will stay high for the next quarter. If you want a good margin, send your quote soon, because a discount today is safer than waiting. Remember to check the currency on every invoice, because the dollar is stronger than the euro right now. That is all for today.

extra_audio.text: First, check the volume the buyer wants and your margin. Next, calculate the rate and prepare a clear quote in the right currency. Then, if the volume is big, offer a small discount on the figure. After that, compare your price with the market to show it is more competitive. Finally, send the invoice and confirm the final bid.
